## Summary: P-Code VM Interpreter Design

Based on my analysis of the p-code VM implementation in `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` and the design documents, here's a comprehensive breakdown:

---

## 1. CALL (0x33) - Procedure Call & Activation Record Setup

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 838-871

```asm
op_call:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    ; Fetch addr24 (target) from code[pc]
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    add r0, r2
    lw r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = target address
    push r2
    ; Return PC = pc + 3 (skip past addr24 operand)
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    add r0, 3
    ; Build frame header on call stack
    lw r2, 6(fp)
    ; r2 = csp
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; frame[0] = return PC
    lw r0, 9(fp)
    sw r0, 3(r2)
    ; frame[3] = dynamic link (current fp_vm)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 6(r2)
    ; frame[6] = static link (0, step 007 adds chain)
    lw r0, 3(fp)
    sw r0, 9(r2)
    ; frame[9] = saved esp
    ; Advance csp by 12 (frame header size)
    add r2, 12
    sw r2, 6(fp)
    ; Set pc = target
    pop r0
    sw r0, 0(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Key Points:**
- Fetches 24-bit target address from code stream
- Computes return PC = current PC + 3
- Pushes frame header (12 bytes) onto call stack at csp:
  - **Offset 0**: Return PC
  - **Offset 3**: Dynamic link (caller's fp_vm for return)
  - **Offset 6**: Static link (0 for `call`, computed for `calln`)
  - **Offset 9**: Saved esp (eval stack pointer)
- Advances csp by 12 bytes (frame header size)
- Sets PC to target address
- Static link is used for accessing variables from lexically enclosing scopes

---

## 2. RET (0x34) - Return from Procedure & Frame Teardown

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 875-955

```asm
op_ret:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    ; Fetch nargs byte, compute nargs * 3
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    add r0, r2
    lbu r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = nargs
    mov r0, r2
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = nargs * 3
    la r2, ret_temps
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; ret_temps[0] = nargs * 3
    ; Get saved_esp from frame header (fp_vm - 3)
    lw r0, 9(fp)
    lw r1, -3(r0)
    ; r1 = saved_esp
    la r2, ret_temps
    sw r1, 3(r2)
    ; ret_temps[3] = saved_esp
    ; Check for return value: esp > saved_esp?
    lw r0, 3(fp)
    ; r0 = current esp
    cls r1, r0
    brf ret_no_rv
    ; Has return value: save TOS
    lw r0, -3(r0)
    ; r0 = retval (eval stack TOS)
    la r2, ret_temps
    sw r0, 6(r2)
    ; ret_temps[6] = retval
    lc r0, 1
    sw r0, 9(r2)
    ; ret_temps[9] = has_rv = 1
    bra ret_restore
ret_no_rv:
    la r2, ret_temps
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 9(r2)
    ; ret_temps[9] = has_rv = 0
ret_restore:
    ; Restore pc from frame header (fp_vm - 12)
    lw r0, 9(fp)
    lw r2, -12(r0)
    sw r2, 0(fp)
    ; pc = return PC
    ; Set csp = fp_vm - 12 (pop entire frame)
    add r0, -12
    sw r0, 6(fp)
    ; Restore fp_vm from dynamic link (fp_vm - 9)
    lw r0, 9(fp)
    lw r2, -9(r0)
    sw r2, 9(fp)
    ; fp_vm = caller's fp_vm
    ; Clean args: esp = saved_esp - nargs * 3
    la r0, ret_temps
    lw r1, 3(r0)
    ; r1 = saved_esp
    lw r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = nargs * 3
    sub r1, r2
    sw r1, 3(fp)
    ; esp = saved_esp - nargs * 3
    ; Push return value if present
    la r0, ret_temps
    lw r2, 9(r0)
    ceq r2, z
    brt ret_done
    ; Push retval onto eval stack
    lw r2, 6(r0)
    ; r2 = retval
    lw r0, 3(fp)
    ; r0 = esp
    sw r2, 0(r0)
    add r0, 3
    sw r0, 3(fp)
ret_done:
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Key Points:**
- Fetches nargs byte (number of arguments to clean)
- Detects return value by comparing esp > saved_esp
- **Frame restoration**:
  - Restores PC from frame[-12]
  - Sets csp = fp_vm - 12 (discards entire frame)
  - Restores fp_vm from frame[-9] (dynamic link)
  - Cleans arguments: esp = saved_esp - nargs*3
- If a return value was pushed (esp > saved_esp), pushes it back onto eval stack
- Uses temporary memory (ret_temps) to hold state during complex operations

---

## 3. Activation Record Layout

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/design.md` section 3

```
         ┌──────────────────┐  ← new csp (after enter)
         │ local[N-1]       │  offset = +(N-1)*3 from fp
         │ ...              │
         │ local[0]         │  offset = 0 from fp
fp ───→  ├──────────────────┤
         │ procedure ID     │  offset = -3
         │ static link      │  offset = -6  (lexically enclosing frame)
         │ dynamic link     │  offset = -9  (caller's fp)
         │ return PC        │  offset = -12
         ├──────────────────┤
         │ arg[N-1]         │  offset = -15  (from fp)
         │ ...              │
         │ arg[0]           │  offset = -15 - (N-1)*3
         └──────────────────┘
```

**Memory Layout:**
- **fp (frame pointer)** points to the first local variable
- **Locals** grow upward from fp (positive offsets 0, 3, 6, ...)
- **Frame header** occupies 12 bytes below fp:
  - **fp - 12**: Return PC
  - **fp - 9**: Dynamic link (previous fp)
  - **fp - 6**: Static link (for nested scope access)
  - **fp - 3**: Procedure ID (reserved, not used in current impl)
- **Arguments** are on eval stack below frame header (accessed via saved_esp)

---

## 4. ENTER (0x40) - Frame Setup & Local Allocation

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 1037-1064

```asm
op_enter:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    ; Fetch nlocals byte
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    add r0, r2
    lbu r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = nlocals
    push r2
    ; Advance pc by 1
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    add r0, 1
    sw r0, 0(fp)
    ; fp_vm = csp
    lw r0, 6(fp)
    sw r0, 9(fp)
    ; csp += nlocals * 3
    pop r2
    ; r2 = nlocals
    mov r0, r2
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = nlocals * 3
    lw r2, 6(fp)
    add r2, r0
    sw r2, 6(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Semantics:**
- Sets **fp_vm = csp** (frame pointer now points to start of locals)
- Reserves space: **csp += nlocals * 3** (each word = 3 bytes)
- ENTER must be called immediately after CALL before accessing locals

---

## 5. LEAVE (0x41) - Frame Teardown

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 1067-1073

```asm
op_leave:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lw r0, 9(fp)
    sw r0, 6(fp)
    ; csp = fp_vm
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Semantics:**
- Simply discards locals: **csp = fp_vm**
- Should be called before RET

---

## 6. Argument Access: LOADA (0x48) & STOREA (0x49)

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 1269-1335

```asm
op_loada:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    add r0, r2
    lbu r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = idx
    ; Advance pc by 1
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    add r0, 1
    sw r0, 0(fp)
    ; Compute (idx + 1) * 3
    add r2, 1
    mov r0, r2
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = (idx + 1) * 3
    ; Get saved_esp from frame (fp_vm - 3)
    lw r2, 9(fp)
    lw r2, -3(r2)
    ; r2 = saved_esp
    sub r2, r0
    ; r2 = arg address
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = arg value
    ; Push onto eval stack
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

op_storea:
    ; ... similar, but pops eval stack and stores to arg location
    ; Compute (idx + 1) * 3
    add r2, 1
    mov r0, r2
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; Get saved_esp from frame (fp_vm - 3)
    lw r2, 9(fp)
    lw r2, -3(r2)
    sub r2, r0
    ; r2 = arg address
```

**Formula**: `arg[idx] = saved_esp - (idx + 1) * 3`

- Arguments are stored on eval stack **below** the saved_esp
- `loada 0` fetches the first argument (oldest pushed)
- Arguments indexed from 0 (left-to-right in source)

---

## 7. I/O Operations - SYS Instruction (0x60)

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 1582-1763

### SYS PUTC (id=1) - Write Byte

```asm
sys_putc:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    add r2, -3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = new esp = &TOS
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = char value
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
sys_putc_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt sys_putc_wait
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( char -- )`
- Pops char from eval stack
- Polls UART status register at 0xFF0101 (address -65280 + 1)
  - Waits for TX ready (status bit 7, checked via sign-extend: `cls r0, z` sets C if negative)
- Writes byte to UART data register at 0xFF0100 (address -65280)

### SYS GETC (id=2) - Read Byte

```asm
sys_getc:
    ; Poll UART status until RX ready (bit 0)
sys_getc_wait:
    la r2, -65280
    lbu r0, 1(r2)
    ; bit 0 = RX ready; mask it
    lc r2, 1
    and r0, r2
    ceq r0, z
    brt sys_getc_wait
    ; RX ready — read data byte
    la r2, -65280
    lbu r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = received byte; push onto eval stack
    la r2, vm_state
    push r2
    pop fp
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = esp
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( -- char )`
- Polls UART status register (bit 0 = RX ready)
- Blocks until data available
- Reads byte from UART data register
- Pushes onto eval stack

### SYS HALT (id=0)

```asm
sys_halt:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lc r0, 1
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( -- )`
- Sets vm_state.status = 1 (halted)
- Returns to vm_loop, which detects halt and exits

### SYS ALLOC (id=4) - Heap Allocation

```asm
sys_alloc:
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp
    ; Pop size from eval stack
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    add r2, -3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = size
    ; Save size to nonlocal_temps[0]
    la r2, nonlocal_temps
    push r2
    pop fp
    sw r0, 0(fp)
    ; Load current hp and save as return pointer to nonlocal_temps[3]
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp
    lw r0, 15(fp)
    ; r0 = old hp (allocated pointer)
    la r2, nonlocal_temps
    push r2
    pop fp
    sw r0, 3(fp)
    ; Compute new hp = old_hp + size
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    ; r2 = size
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = new hp
    ; Store new hp to vm_state.hp
    la r2, vm_state
    push r2
    pop fp
    sw r0, 15(fp)
    ; Push old hp (allocated pointer) onto eval stack
    la r2, nonlocal_temps
    push r2
    pop fp
    lw r0, 3(fp)
    ; r0 = allocated pointer
    la r2, vm_state
    push r2
    pop fp
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = esp
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( size -- ptr )`
- Pops size (in words) from eval stack
- Uses bump allocator: **new_ptr = old_hp**, **hp += size**
- Pushes allocated pointer onto eval stack
- Returns old heap pointer as the allocated block

### System Call IDs

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED D2 state (active-low inverted) |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |
| 6 | READ_SWITCH | ( -- state ) | Read button S2 state (active-low inverted) |

---

## 8. Local & Global Variable Access

**LOADL (0x42)**: `op_loadl` lines 1079-1108
- **Address**: `fp_vm + offset * 3`
- **Formula**: Local variable at index offset

**STOREL (0x43)**: `op_storel` lines 1110-1141
- Pops eval stack into local variable

**LOADG (0x44)**: `op_loadg` lines 1143-1172
- **Address**: `gp + offset * 3`
- **Formula**: Global variable at index offset

**STOREG (0x45)**: `op_storeg` lines 1174-1206
- Pops eval stack into global variable

---

## 9. Memory Layout

**VM State struct** (at fixed address vm_state): 
- Offset 0: **pc** (program counter)
- Offset 3: **esp** (eval stack pointer)
- Offset 6: **csp** (call stack pointer)
- Offset 9: **fp_vm** (current frame pointer)
- Offset 12: **gp** (globals base)
- Offset 15: **hp** (heap pointer)
- Offset 18: **code** (code segment base)
- Offset 21: **status** (0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped)
- Offset 24: **trap_code** (last trap code)

All pointers are word addresses, word size = 3 bytes (COR24 word)

---

## 10. Calling Convention Example

From test file `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/tests/merged_21procs.spc`:

```pasm
.proc main 0
    push 42
    call _p24p_write_int
    call _p24p_write_ln
    halt
.end

.proc _p24p_write_int 1      ; 1 local variable
    loada 0                   ; load arg 0 (the value)
    ; ... operations ...
    storel 0                  ; store result in local 0
    loadl 0                   ; load local 0
    ; ... use value ...
    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 argument
.end
```

**Sequence**:
1. Caller: `push 42` (argument on eval stack)
2. Caller: `call _p24p_write_int` (sets pc to target, builds frame header)
3. Callee: `enter 1` (reserves 1 local, sets fp_vm = csp)
4. Callee: `loada 0` (loads arg 0 from saved_esp)
5. Callee: `storel 0` (stores into local 0 at fp_vm + 0)
6. Callee: `leave` (discards locals)
7. Callee: `ret 1` (cleans 1 arg, restores pc/fp/esp)

## Summary: P-Code VM Interpreter Design

Based on my analysis of the p-code VM implementation in `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` and the design documents, here's a comprehensive breakdown:

---

## 1. CALL (0x33) - Procedure Call & Activation Record Setup

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 838-871

```asm
op_call:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    ; Fetch addr24 (target) from code[pc]
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    add r0, r2
    lw r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = target address
    push r2
    ; Return PC = pc + 3 (skip past addr24 operand)
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    add r0, 3
    ; Build frame header on call stack
    lw r2, 6(fp)
    ; r2 = csp
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    ; frame[0] = return PC
    lw r0, 9(fp)
    sw r0, 3(r2)
    ; frame[3] = dynamic link (current fp_vm)
    lc r0, 0
    sw r0, 6(r2)
    ; frame[6] = static link (0, step 007 adds chain)
    lw r0, 3(fp)
    sw r0, 9(r2)
    ; frame[9] = saved esp
    ; Advance csp by 12 (frame header size)
    add r2, 12
    sw r2, 6(fp)
    ; Set pc = target
    pop r0
    sw r0, 0(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Key Points:**
- Fetches 24-bit target address from code stream
- Computes return PC = current PC + 3
- Pushes frame header (12 bytes) onto call stack at csp:
  - **Offset 0**: Return PC
  - **Offset 3**: Dynamic link (caller's fp_vm for return)
  - **Offset 6**: Static link (0 for `call`, computed for `calln`)
  - **Offset 9**: Saved esp (eval stack pointer)
- Advances csp by 12 bytes (frame header size)
- Sets PC to target address
- Static link is used for accessing variables from lexically enclosing scopes

---

- Should be called before RET

## 6. Argument Access: LOADA (0x48) & STOREA (0x49)

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 1269-1335

```asm
op_loada:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lw r0, 18(fp)
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    add r0, r2
    lbu r2, 0(r0)
    ; r2 = idx
    ; Advance pc by 1
    lw r0, 0(fp)
    add r0, 1
    sw r0, 0(fp)
    ; Compute (idx + 1) * 3
    add r2, 1
    mov r0, r2
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = (idx + 1) * 3
    ; Get saved_esp from frame (fp_vm - 3)
    lw r2, 9(fp)
    lw r2, -3(r2)
    ; r2 = saved_esp
    sub r2, r0
    ; r2 = arg address
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = arg value
    ; Push onto eval stack
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)

op_storea:
    ; ... similar, but pops eval stack and stores to arg location
    ; Compute (idx + 1) * 3
    add r2, 1
    mov r0, r2
    add r0, r0
    add r0, r2
    ; Get saved_esp from frame (fp_vm - 3)
    lw r2, 9(fp)
    lw r2, -3(r2)
    sub r2, r0
    ; r2 = arg address
```

**Formula**: `arg[idx] = saved_esp - (idx + 1) * 3`

- Arguments are stored on eval stack **below** the saved_esp
- `loada 0` fetches the first argument (oldest pushed)
- Arguments indexed from 0 (left-to-right in source)

---

## 7. I/O Operations - SYS Instruction (0x60)

**Source**: `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s` lines 1582-1763

### SYS PUTC (id=1) - Write Byte

```asm
sys_putc:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    add r2, -3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = new esp = &TOS
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = char value
    push r0
    la r2, -65280
sys_putc_wait:
    lb r0, 1(r2)
    cls r0, z
    brt sys_putc_wait
    pop r0
    sb r0, 0(r2)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( char -- )`
- Pops char from eval stack
- Polls UART status register at 0xFF0101 (address -65280 + 1)
  - Waits for TX ready (status bit 7, checked via sign-extend: `cls r0, z` sets C if negative)
- Writes byte to UART data register at 0xFF0100 (address -65280)

### SYS GETC (id=2) - Read Byte

```asm
sys_getc:
    ; Poll UART status until RX ready (bit 0)
sys_getc_wait:
    la r2, -65280
    lbu r0, 1(r2)
    ; bit 0 = RX ready; mask it
    lc r2, 1
    and r0, r2
    ceq r0, z
    brt sys_getc_wait
    ; RX ready — read data byte
    la r2, -65280
    lbu r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = received byte; push onto eval stack
    la r2, vm_state
    push r2
    pop fp
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = esp
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( -- char )`
- Polls UART status register (bit 0 = RX ready)
- Blocks until data available
- Reads byte from UART data register
- Pushes onto eval stack

### SYS HALT (id=0)

```asm
sys_halt:
    ; fp = &vm_state
    lc r0, 1
    sw r0, 21(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( -- )`
- Sets vm_state.status = 1 (halted)
- Returns to vm_loop, which detects halt and exits

### SYS ALLOC (id=4) - Heap Allocation

```asm
sys_alloc:
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp
    ; Pop size from eval stack
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    add r2, -3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    lw r0, 0(r2)
    ; r0 = size
    ; Save size to nonlocal_temps[0]
    la r2, nonlocal_temps
    push r2
    pop fp
    sw r0, 0(fp)
    ; Load current hp and save as return pointer to nonlocal_temps[3]
    la r0, vm_state
    push r0
    pop fp
    lw r0, 15(fp)
    ; r0 = old hp (allocated pointer)
    la r2, nonlocal_temps
    push r2
    pop fp
    sw r0, 3(fp)
    ; Compute new hp = old_hp + size
    lw r2, 0(fp)
    ; r2 = size
    add r0, r2
    ; r0 = new hp
    ; Store new hp to vm_state.hp
    la r2, vm_state
    push r2
    pop fp
    sw r0, 15(fp)
    ; Push old hp (allocated pointer) onto eval stack
    la r2, nonlocal_temps
    push r2
    pop fp
    lw r0, 3(fp)
    ; r0 = allocated pointer
    la r2, vm_state
    push r2
    pop fp
    lw r2, 3(fp)
    ; r2 = esp
    sw r0, 0(r2)
    add r2, 3
    sw r2, 3(fp)
    la r0, vm_loop
    jmp (r0)
```

**Stack Effect**: `( size -- ptr )`
- Pops size (in words) from eval stack
- Uses bump allocator: **new_ptr = old_hp**, **hp += size**
- Pushes allocated pointer onto eval stack
- Returns old heap pointer as the allocated block

### System Call IDs

| ID | Name | Stack Effect | Description |
|----|------|-------------|-------------|
| 0 | HALT | ( -- ) | Stop execution |
| 1 | PUTC | ( char -- ) | Write byte to UART |
| 2 | GETC | ( -- char ) | Read byte from UART (blocking) |
| 3 | LED | ( state -- ) | Write LED D2 state (active-low inverted) |
| 4 | ALLOC | ( size -- ptr ) | Bump-allocate heap block |
| 5 | FREE | ( ptr -- ) | Free heap block (no-op in bump mode) |
| 6 | READ_SWITCH | ( -- state ) | Read button S2 state (active-low inverted) |

---

## 8. Local & Global Variable Access

**LOADL (0x42)**: `op_loadl` lines 1079-1108
- **Address**: `fp_vm + offset * 3`
- **Formula**: Local variable at index offset

**STOREL (0x43)**: `op_storel` lines 1110-1141
- Pops eval stack into local variable

**LOADG (0x44)**: `op_loadg` lines 1143-1172
- **Address**: `gp + offset * 3`
- **Formula**: Global variable at index offset

**STOREG (0x45)**: `op_storeg` lines 1174-1206
- Pops eval stack into global variable

---

## 9. Memory Layout

**VM State struct** (at fixed address vm_state): 
- Offset 0: **pc** (program counter)
- Offset 3: **esp** (eval stack pointer)
- Offset 6: **csp** (call stack pointer)
- Offset 9: **fp_vm** (current frame pointer)
- Offset 12: **gp** (globals base)
- Offset 15: **hp** (heap pointer)
- Offset 18: **code** (code segment base)
- Offset 21: **status** (0=running, 1=halted, 2=trapped)
- Offset 24: **trap_code** (last trap code)

All pointers are word addresses, word size = 3 bytes (COR24 word)

---

## 10. Calling Convention Example

From test file `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-pcode/vm/tests/merged_21procs.spc`:

```pasm
.proc main 0
    push 42
    call _p24p_write_int
    call _p24p_write_ln
    halt
.end

.proc _p24p_write_int 1      ; 1 local variable
    loada 0                   ; load arg 0 (the value)
    ; ... operations ...
    storel 0                  ; store result in local 0
    loadl 0                   ; load local 0
    ; ... use value ...
    ret 1                     ; return, clean 1 argument
.end
```

**Sequence**:
1. Caller: `push 42` (argument on eval stack)
2. Caller: `call _p24p_write_int` (sets pc to target, builds frame header)
3. Callee: `enter 1` (reserves 1 local, sets fp_vm = csp)
4. Callee: `loada 0` (loads arg 0 from saved_esp)
5. Callee: `storel 0` (stores into local 0 at fp_vm + 0)
6. Callee: `leave` (discards locals)
7. Callee: `ret 1` (cleans 1 arg, restores pc/fp/esp)

## COR24 Assembly Language Reference - Instruction Set & Procedure Calls

Based on my analysis of the COR24 ISA, assembler, and emulator code, here's the complete technical reference:

### **REGISTER SET (3-bit encoding, 0-7)**

```
r0, r1, r2     General-purpose (24-bit)
r3 (fp)        Frame pointer
r4 (sp)        Stack pointer (initialized to 0xFEEC00)
r5 (z/c)       Zero register / Condition flag (read-only for arithmetic)
r6 (iv)        Interrupt vector
r7 (ir)        Interrupt return address
```

**Note:** r1 is **reserved for return addresses** in subroutine calls and must not be used for other purposes.

---

### **COMPLETE INSTRUCTION SET (32 operations)**

| Category | Instructions | Format | Examples |
|----------|------------|--------|----------|
| **Arithmetic** | `add`, `sub`, `mul` | add ra,rb / add ra,imm8 | add r0,r1 / add r0,5 |
| **Logic** | `and`, `or`, `xor` | ra,rb | and r0,r1 |
| **Shifts** | `shl`, `sra`, `srl` | ra,rb | shl r0,r1 |
| **Compare** | `ceq`, `cls`, `clu` | ra,rb (sets C flag) | ceq r0,r1 |
| **Branch** | `bra`, `brf`, `brt` | offset8 | bra loop_label |
| **Jump** | `jmp`, `jal` | (ra) / ra,(rb) | jmp (r0) / jal r1,(r2) |
| **Load Addr** | `la` | ra,addr24 | la r0,func_addr |
| **Load Imm** | `lc`, `lcu` | ra,imm8 | lc r0,42 |
| **Load Mem** | `lb`, `lbu`, `lw` | ra,offset8(rb) | lw r0,4(fp) |
| **Store** | `sb`, `sw` | ra,offset8(rb) | sw r0,8(sp) |
| **Stack** | `push`, `pop` | ra (reg,sp) | push r0 / pop r1 |
| **Move** | `mov`, `sxt`, `zxt` | ra,rb | mov r0,r1 |
| **Special** | `sub sp,imm24` | stack adjust | sub sp,256 |

---

### **SUBROUTINE CALL/RETURN MECHANISM**

COR24 uses **register-based linking** rather than a dedicated call stack instruction:

#### **Calling a Subroutine**

```assembly
; Pattern 1: Call with known address
la      r2, target_func      ; Load function address (4 bytes)
jal     r1, (r2)             ; Jump to r2, store return addr in r1
                             ; (next_pc is automatically computed)

; Pattern 2: Call with indirect address (via register)
jal     r1, (r0)             ; Jump to address in r0, return to r1

; Pattern 3: Direct jump (same register encoding exists)
```

**How JAL works:**
- `jal ra,(rb)` — Jump to address in rb, store return address (next_pc) in ra
- Return address = address of next instruction after jal
- With 1-byte JAL: next_pc = current_pc + 1
- With 4-byte LA preceding: functions are in different code sections

#### **Returning from Subroutine**

```assembly
jmp     (r1)                 ; Jump to return address (set by caller's jal)
```

This clears the interrupt-in-service flag if using r7.

#### **Complete Example from demo_nested.cor24.s:**

```assembly
delay:
    sub     sp, 3            ; Allocate locals
    ceq     r0, z
    brt     .LBB2_3
    add     r0, -1
.LBB2_2:
    mov     r2, sp
    sw      r0, 0(r2)
    add     r0, -1
    lc      r1, -1
    ceq     r0, r1
    brf     .LBB2_2
.LBB2_3:
    add     sp, 3            ; Deallocate locals
    pop     r2               ; Restore r2 (callee-saved)
    jmp     (r2)             ; Return to caller

; Calling delay:
    la      r2, .Lret_7      ; Load return label
    push    r2               ; Push return address on stack
    la      r2, delay        ; Load function address
    jmp     (r2)             ; Jump (r1 is not used here)
    .Lret_7:                 ; Return point
    ...
```

**Key difference:** In the translator output, return addresses are pushed to the stack and popped/returned via `pop r2; jmp (r2)`. This is MSP430→COR24 translation that avoids using r1 for return addresses within nested calls.

The native COR24 mechanism uses:
```assembly
push    r1                   ; Save caller's return address
la      r2, target
jal     r1, (r2)             ; Set r1 to next instruction
pop     r1                   ; Restore caller's return address
```

---

### **STACK & FRAME POINTER**

```
sp (r4)   Stack pointer - grows downward from 0xFEEC00
fp (r3)   Frame pointer - points to current frame
          Convention: locals at negative offsets from fp

Frame layout:
    fp+0:   [frame pointer of caller]
    fp+3:   [local variable 1]
    fp+6:   [local variable 2]
    ...
    sp:     [top of stack]
```

**Stack operations:**
- `push ra` — sp -= 3, write ra as 24-bit word to [sp]
- `pop ra` — read 24-bit word from [sp] to ra, sp += 3
- `sub sp, imm24` — Allocate stack space (sp -= imm24)

---

### **INSTRUCTION ENCODING FORMAT**

COR24 has **variable-length instructions** (1, 2, or 4 bytes):

**1-byte instructions (opcode + registers):**
- First byte encodes opcode (5 bits) + ra (3 bits) + rb (3 bits)
- Examples: `add r0,r1`, `jmp (r2)`, `push r0`

**2-byte instructions (opcode + imm8):**
- First byte: opcode + ra (for imm operations)
- Second byte: signed/unsigned 8-bit immediate
- Examples: `add r0,5`, `lb r0,10(r1)`, `bra label`

**4-byte instructions (opcode + imm24):**
- First byte: opcode + ra
- Bytes 1-3: 24-bit address (little-endian)
- Examples: `la r0,0x123456`, `sub sp,256`

**Encoding table excerpt:**
```rust
// From cor24-rs/isa/src/encode.rs
Opcode::Push => match (ra, rb) {
    (0, 4) => Some(0x7D),   // push r0
    (1, 4) => Some(0x7E),   // push r1
    (2, 4) => Some(0x7F),   // push r2
    (3, 4) => Some(0x80),   // push fp
    _ => None,
},
Opcode::Pop => match (ra, rb) {
    (0, 4) => Some(0x79),   // pop r0
    (1, 4) => Some(0x7A),   // pop r1
    (2, 4) => Some(0x7B),   // pop r2
    (3, 4) => Some(0x7C),   // pop fp
    _ => None,
},
Opcode::Jal => match (ra, rb) {
    (1, 0) => Some(0x25),   // jal r1,(r0)
    (1, 1) => Some(0xD1),   // jal r1,(r1)
    (1, 2) => Some(0xD2),   // jal r1,(r2)
    _ => None,
},
Opcode::Jmp => match (ra, rb) {
    (0, 7) => Some(0x26),   // jmp (r0)
    (1, 7) => Some(0x27),   // jmp (r1)
    (2, 7) => Some(0x28),   // jmp (r2)
    (7, 7) => Some(0x68),   // jmp (r7) — clears intis
    _ => None,
},
```

**Only supported register combinations:** Many register combinations are **not available**. For example, there's no `push r4` (sp) or `pop r5` (z). The assembler enforces this.

---

### **UART OUTPUT (Memory-Mapped I/O)**

**Addresses (24-bit):**
```
0xFF0100    UART Data Register (write to transmit)
0xFF0101    UART Status Register (read/write)
```

**Status bits:**
```
bit 0       RX data ready
bit 1       CTS (clear-to-send) active
bit 2       RX overflow
bit 7       TX busy (0=ready, 1=busy)
```

**Example UART write:**
```assembly
; Write character in r0 to UART
la      r2, 0xFF0100         ; Load UART data address
sb      r0, 0(r2)            ; Store low byte to UART
```

**Poll-before-write (wait until TX ready):**
```assembly
poll_tx:
    la      r2, 0xFF0101     ; Load UART status address
    lb      r1, 0(r2)        ; Read status (signed)
    lc      r0, 128          ; TX busy bit (0x80 = bit 7)
    and     r1, r0           ; Check if busy
    brt     poll_tx          ; If set, busy—loop
    ; Now TX is ready, write data
    sb      r0, -1(r2)       ; Store to UART data (0xFF0100)
```

**From emulator source code:**
```rust
// IO addresses mapping
pub const IO_UARTDATA: u32 = 0xFF0100;  // UART data
pub const IO_UARTSTAT: u32 = 0xFF0101;  // UART status
pub const IO_LEDSWDAT: u32 = 0xFF0000;  // LED/Switch

// From msp430.rs translator:
-255 => 0x00FF0100_u32 as i32,  // 0xFF01 → UART data
-254 => 0x00FF0101_u32 as i32,  // 0xFF02 → UART status
```

---

### **CPU EXECUTION MODEL**

**From executor.rs:**
```rust
// JAL: Jump and Link (opcode 0x09)
Opcode::Jal => {
    let target = cpu.get_reg(inst.rb);
    cpu.set_reg(inst.ra, next_pc);      // Store return address
    cpu.pc = target;
}

// JMP: Unconditional Jump (opcode 0x0A)
Opcode::Jmp => {
    let target = cpu.get_reg(inst.ra);
    if inst.ra == 7 {
        cpu.intis = false;               // Clear interrupt-in-service if jmp (r7)
    }
    cpu.pc = target;
}

// PUSH (opcode 0x15)
// sp -= 3, write ra as 24-bit word
cpu.set_reg(4, sp - 3);
cpu.write_word(sp - 3, value);

// POP (opcode 0x14)
// read 24-bit word from [sp], sp += 3
let value = cpu.read_word(sp);
cpu.set_reg(4, sp + 3);
```

**Instruction pipeline:** Branches and jumps have a 2-cycle delay in the hardware (pipeline flush), but the emulator abstracts this away. For assembly purposes, just follow the patterns above.

---

### **ASSEMBLER MNEMONICS SUMMARY**

The assembler supports all mnemonics listed in the instruction table above. Use lowercase. Examples:
```assembly
add r0,r1           ; Add r0 + r1 -> r0
la r0,0x1000        ; Load address into r0
lc r1,42            ; Load constant 42 into r1
lw r0,4(fp)         ; Load word from fp+4
sw r0,8(sp)         ; Store word at sp+8
bra loop             ; Branch always to label 'loop'
ceq r0,r1           ; Compare equal: C = (r0 == r1)
brt target          ; Branch if true (C=1)
push r0             ; Push r0 onto stack
pop r1              ; Pop from stack to r1
```

---

### **KEY CONSTRAINTS FOR CODE GENERATION**

1. **r1 is reserved** for return addresses in JAL/JMP-based calls
2. **Only specific (ra,rb) pairs exist** for each opcode — encoder returns None for unsupported pairs
3. **Push/pop only support r0, r1, r2, r3** (fp) with sp (r4)
4. **Condition flag (r5)** is read-only except via `mov ra,c` to copy it
5. **Stack grows downward**, sp starts at 0xFEEC00
6. **24-bit address space**, max address 0xFFFFFF
7. **Active-low LED/switch** at 0xFF0000 (0=active, 1=inactive)

---

### **KEY FILES**

- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa/src/opcode.rs` — Opcode definitions
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa/src/encode.rs` — Instruction encoding tables (canonical source)
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator/src/cpu/executor.rs` — CPU execution semantics
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator/src/cpu/state.rs` — Memory map, I/O addresses
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler/src/assembler.rs` — Assembler implementation

    .Lret_7:                 ; Return point
    ...
```

**Key difference:** In the translator output, return addresses are pushed to the stack and popped/returned via `pop r2; jmp (r2)`. This is MSP430→COR24 translation that avoids using r1 for return addresses within nested calls.

The native COR24 mechanism uses:
```assembly
push    r1                   ; Save caller's return address
la      r2, target
jal     r1, (r2)             ; Set r1 to next instruction
pop     r1                   ; Restore caller's return address
```

---

### **STACK & FRAME POINTER**

```
sp (r4)   Stack pointer - grows downward from 0xFEEC00
fp (r3)   Frame pointer - points to current frame
          Convention: locals at negative offsets from fp

Frame layout:
    fp+0:   [frame pointer of caller]
    fp+3:   [local variable 1]
    fp+6:   [local variable 2]
    ...
    sp:     [top of stack]
```

**Stack operations:**
- `push ra` — sp -= 3, write ra as 24-bit word to [sp]
- `pop ra` — read 24-bit word from [sp] to ra, sp += 3
- `sub sp, imm24` — Allocate stack space (sp -= imm24)

---

### **INSTRUCTION ENCODING FORMAT**

COR24 has **variable-length instructions** (1, 2, or 4 bytes):

**1-byte instructions (opcode + registers):**
- First byte encodes opcode (5 bits) + ra (3 bits) + rb (3 bits)
- Examples: `add r0,r1`, `jmp (r2)`, `push r0`

**2-byte instructions (opcode + imm8):**
- First byte: opcode + ra (for imm operations)
- Second byte: signed/unsigned 8-bit immediate
- Examples: `add r0,5`, `lb r0,10(r1)`, `bra label`

**4-byte instructions (opcode + imm24):**
- First byte: opcode + ra
- Bytes 1-3: 24-bit address (little-endian)
- Examples: `la r0,0x123456`, `sub sp,256`

**Encoding table excerpt:**
```rust
// From cor24-rs/isa/src/encode.rs
Opcode::Push => match (ra, rb) {
    (0, 4) => Some(0x7D),   // push r0
    (1, 4) => Some(0x7E),   // push r1
    (2, 4) => Some(0x7F),   // push r2
    (3, 4) => Some(0x80),   // push fp
    _ => None,
},
Opcode::Pop => match (ra, rb) {
    (0, 4) => Some(0x79),   // pop r0
    (1, 4) => Some(0x7A),   // pop r1
    (2, 4) => Some(0x7B),   // pop r2
    (3, 4) => Some(0x7C),   // pop fp
    _ => None,
},
Opcode::Jal => match (ra, rb) {
    (1, 0) => Some(0x25),   // jal r1,(r0)
    (1, 1) => Some(0xD1),   // jal r1,(r1)
    (1, 2) => Some(0xD2),   // jal r1,(r2)
    _ => None,
},
Opcode::Jmp => match (ra, rb) {
    (0, 7) => Some(0x26),   // jmp (r0)
    (1, 7) => Some(0x27),   // jmp (r1)
    (2, 7) => Some(0x28),   // jmp (r2)
    (7, 7) => Some(0x68),   // jmp (r7) — clears intis
    _ => None,
},
```

**Only supported register combinations:** Many register combinations are **not available**. For example, there's no `push r4` (sp) or `pop r5` (z). The assembler enforces this.

---

### **UART OUTPUT (Memory-Mapped I/O)**

**Addresses (24-bit):**
```
0xFF0100    UART Data Register (write to transmit)
0xFF0101    UART Status Register (read/write)
```

**Status bits:**
```
bit 0       RX data ready
bit 1       CTS (clear-to-send) active
bit 2       RX overflow
bit 7       TX busy (0=ready, 1=busy)
```

**Example UART write:**
```assembly
; Write character in r0 to UART
la      r2, 0xFF0100         ; Load UART data address
sb      r0, 0(r2)            ; Store low byte to UART
```

**Poll-before-write (wait until TX ready):**
```assembly
poll_tx:
    la      r2, 0xFF0101     ; Load UART status address
    lb      r1, 0(r2)        ; Read status (signed)
    lc      r0, 128          ; TX busy bit (0x80 = bit 7)
    and     r1, r0           ; Check if busy
    brt     poll_tx          ; If set, busy—loop
    ; Now TX is ready, write data
    sb      r0, -1(r2)       ; Store to UART data (0xFF0100)
```

**From emulator source code:**
```rust
// IO addresses mapping
pub const IO_UARTDATA: u32 = 0xFF0100;  // UART data
pub const IO_UARTSTAT: u32 = 0xFF0101;  // UART status
pub const IO_LEDSWDAT: u32 = 0xFF0000;  // LED/Switch

// From msp430.rs translator:
-255 => 0x00FF0100_u32 as i32,  // 0xFF01 → UART data
-254 => 0x00FF0101_u32 as i32,  // 0xFF02 → UART status
```

---

### **CPU EXECUTION MODEL**

**From executor.rs:**
```rust
// JAL: Jump and Link (opcode 0x09)
Opcode::Jal => {
    let target = cpu.get_reg(inst.rb);
    cpu.set_reg(inst.ra, next_pc);      // Store return address
    cpu.pc = target;
}

// JMP: Unconditional Jump (opcode 0x0A)
Opcode::Jmp => {
    let target = cpu.get_reg(inst.ra);
    if inst.ra == 7 {
        cpu.intis = false;               // Clear interrupt-in-service if jmp (r7)
    }
    cpu.pc = target;
}

// PUSH (opcode 0x15)
// sp -= 3, write ra as 24-bit word
cpu.set_reg(4, sp - 3);
cpu.write_word(sp - 3, value);

// POP (opcode 0x14)
// read 24-bit word from [sp], sp += 3
let value = cpu.read_word(sp);
cpu.set_reg(4, sp + 3);
```

**Instruction pipeline:** Branches and jumps have a 2-cycle delay in the hardware (pipeline flush), but the emulator abstracts this away. For assembly purposes, just follow the patterns above.

---

### **ASSEMBLER MNEMONICS SUMMARY**

The assembler supports all mnemonics listed in the instruction table above. Use lowercase. Examples:
```assembly
add r0,r1           ; Add r0 + r1 -> r0
la r0,0x1000        ; Load address into r0
lc r1,42            ; Load constant 42 into r1
lw r0,4(fp)         ; Load word from fp+4
sw r0,8(sp)         ; Store word at sp+8
bra loop             ; Branch always to label 'loop'
ceq r0,r1           ; Compare equal: C = (r0 == r1)
brt target          ; Branch if true (C=1)
push r0             ; Push r0 onto stack
pop r1              ; Pop from stack to r1
```

---

### **KEY CONSTRAINTS FOR CODE GENERATION**

1. **r1 is reserved** for return addresses in JAL/JMP-based calls
2. **Only specific (ra,rb) pairs exist** for each opcode — encoder returns None for unsupported pairs
3. **Push/pop only support r0, r1, r2, r3** (fp) with sp (r4)
4. **Condition flag (r5)** is read-only except via `mov ra,c` to copy it
5. **Stack grows downward**, sp starts at 0xFEEC00
6. **24-bit address space**, max address 0xFFFFFF
7. **Active-low LED/switch** at 0xFF0000 (0=active, 1=inactive)

---

### **KEY FILES**

- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa/src/opcode.rs` — Opcode definitions
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/cor24-rs/isa/src/encode.rs` — Instruction encoding tables (canonical source)
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator/src/cpu/executor.rs` — CPU execution semantics
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-emulator/src/cpu/state.rs` — Memory map, I/O addresses
- `/Users/mike/github/sw-embed/sw-cor24-x-assembler/src/assembler.rs` — Assembler implementation
Good, I have comprehensive context. Let me read the current emitter and the ABI doc to understand exact offsets before implementing.

1	# COR24 ABI for AOT-Compiled P-Code
2	
3	Defines the binary interface between AOT-compiled Pascal programs and
4	the COR24 runtime. This is the contract that both the AOT compiler and
5	runtime helpers must respect.
6	
7	Source: `sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s`, `sw-cor24-pcode/vm/design.md`,
8	`sw-cor24-pcode/vm/architecture.md`
9	
10	## COR24 Register Assignments
11	
12	| Register | Name | AOT Purpose |
13	|----------|------|-------------|
14	| r0 | -- | TOS cache / scratch / return value |
15	| r1 | -- | NOS cache / scratch |
16	| r2 | -- | Scratch |
17	| r3 | fp | Frame pointer (points to current activation record) |
18	| r4 | sp | Stack pointer (COR24 hardware stack in EBR) |
19	| r5 | z | Zero register (always 0, read-only) |
20	| r6 | iv | Interrupt vector (reserved) |
21	| r7 | ir | Interrupt return (reserved) |
22	
23	### Register Convention
24	
25	- **r0-r2**: Caller-saved. Any call may destroy them.
26	- **r3 (fp)**: Callee-saved. Callee must restore before return.
27	- **r4 (sp)**: Callee-saved (balanced push/pop).
28	- **r5 (z)**: Read-only zero. Never written.
29	- **r6-r7**: Reserved for interrupt handling. Never used by compiled code.
30	
31	### TOS Caching Strategy
32	
33	The interpreter uses a memory-based eval stack. The AOT compiler should
34	use TOS caching to avoid excessive memory traffic:
35	
36	- **r0**: Always holds TOS when the eval stack is non-empty
37	- **r1**: Optionally holds NOS for binary operations
38	- **r2**: Available as scratch for address computation
39	
40	When the eval stack depth exceeds 2, spill to the memory-based eval
41	stack pointed to by the eval stack pointer (a dedicated memory
42	location, not a COR24 register).
43	
44	## Activation Record Layout
45	
46	The call stack grows upward (toward higher addresses). Each procedure
47	call creates an activation record (frame) on the call stack.
48	
49	```
50	HIGHER ADDRESSES (call stack grows up)
51	
52	                +-------------------+
53	                | local[N-1]        |  fp_vm + (N-1)*3
54	                | ...               |
55	                | local[0]          |  fp_vm + 0
56	   fp_vm -----> +-------------------+
57	                | saved_esp         |  fp_vm - 3    (eval stack at call time)
58	                | dynamic_link      |  fp_vm - 6    (caller's fp_vm)
59	                | static_link       |  fp_vm - 9    (enclosing scope's fp_vm)
60	                | return_pc         |  fp_vm - 12   (return address)
61	                +-------------------+
62	                | arg[N-1]          |  saved_esp - N*3
63	                | ...               |
64	                | arg[0]            |  saved_esp - 3
65	                | [eval stack TOS]  |  saved_esp
66	                +-------------------+
67	
68	LOWER ADDRESSES
69	```
70	
71	### Frame Header (12 bytes)
72	
73	| Offset from fp_vm | Size | Field | Description |
74	|--------------------|------|-------|-------------|
75	| -12 | 3 bytes | return_pc | P-code address to resume after return |
76	| -9 | 3 bytes | static_link | Frame pointer of lexically enclosing scope |
77	| -6 | 3 bytes | dynamic_link | Caller's frame pointer (for unwinding) |
78	| -3 | 3 bytes | saved_esp | Eval stack pointer at time of call |
79	
80	Note: The interpreter stores these as 4-byte values (word-aligned
81	reads with `lw`/`sw`), but only the low 3 bytes (24 bits) carry data.
82	For AOT compilation targeting native COR24, these should be 3 bytes
83	(one COR24 word) each.
84	
85	### Local Variables
86	
87	Local variables occupy slots starting at fp_vm, growing upward.
88	Each local is one word (3 bytes). Accessed as:
89	
90	```
91	local[i] = mem[fp_vm + i * 3]
92	```
93	
94	### Argument Access
95	
96	Arguments are pushed by the caller onto the eval stack before the call.
97	The callee accesses them relative to saved_esp:
98	
99	```
100	arg[i] = mem[saved_esp - (i + 1) * 3]
101	```
102	
103	Where i=0 is the first argument (pushed first, deepest on stack).
104	
105	## Calling Convention
106	
107	### Call Sequence
108	
109	1. **Caller** pushes arguments left-to-right onto the eval stack
110	2. **Caller** executes `call target`:
111	   - Writes frame header to call stack (return_pc, static_link,
112	     dynamic_link, saved_esp)
113	   - Advances call stack pointer by 12 bytes
114	   - Jumps to target
115	3. **Callee** executes `enter nlocals`:
116	   - Sets fp_vm = call stack pointer
117	   - Advances call stack pointer by nlocals * 3
118	4. **Callee** does work, accessing args via `loada`, locals via `loadl`
119	5. **Callee** pushes return value (if function) onto eval stack
120	6. **Callee** executes `ret nargs`:
121	   - Detects return value: if eval stack pointer > saved_esp
122	   - Saves return value if present
123	   - Restores return_pc, dynamic_link from frame header
124	   - Pops entire frame from call stack
125	   - Cleans arguments: eval stack pointer = saved_esp - nargs * 3
126	   - Pushes return value back if present
127	   - Jumps to return_pc
128	
129	### Static Link Setup
130	
131	For nested procedure calls (`calln depth addr`):
132	- `depth=0`: static_link = current fp_vm (calling a directly nested proc)
133	- `depth=1`: static_link = current frame's static_link (calling a sibling)
134	- `depth>1`: follow static chain (depth-1) times
135	
136	### Nonlocal Variable Access
137	
138	To access a variable in an enclosing scope (`loadn depth off`):
139	
140	```
141	frame = fp_vm
142	repeat depth times:
143	    frame = mem[frame - 9]    // follow static_link
144	result = mem[frame + off * 3]
145	```
146	
147	## Memory Regions
148	
149	### COR24 Address Space (1 MB SRAM + I/O)
150	
151	```
152	0x000000  +------------------------+
153	          | Code segment           |  Native COR24 instructions
154	          | (AOT-compiled program  |  (replaces p-code bytecode)
155	          |  + runtime helpers)    |
156	          +------------------------+
157	          | Read-only data         |  String literals, constants
158	          | (.data sections)       |
159	          +------------------------+
160	          | Global variables       |  One word per .global declaration
161	          +------------------------+
162	          | Call stack             |  Activation records (grows up)
163	          +------------------------+
164	          | Eval stack             |  Expression temporaries (grows up)
165	          +------------------------+
166	          | Heap                   |  Dynamic allocation (grows up)
167	          +------------------------+
168	          | Free SRAM              |
169	          +------------------------+
170	0x0FFFFF  | End of SRAM (1 MB)     |
171	          +------------------------+
172	0xFEEC00  | EBR (3 KB)             |  COR24 hardware stack (sp/r4)
173	0xFEF7FF  +------------------------+
174	0xFF0000  | LED/Switch I/O         |  Write: LED D2; Read: button S2
175	0xFF0100  | UART data              |  TX/RX byte
176	0xFF0101  | UART status            |  Bit 7: TX busy; Bit 0: RX ready
177	```
178	
179	### Segment Sizing
180	
181	The interpreter allocates small fixed segments:
182	- Globals: 8 words (24 bytes)
183	- Call stack: 32 words (96 bytes)
184	- Eval stack: 32 words (96 bytes)
185	- Heap: 32 words (96 bytes)
186	
187	For AOT-compiled programs, segment sizes should be configurable.
188	Reasonable defaults for a 1 MB address space:
189	- Call stack: 256-1024 words
190	- Eval stack: 128-512 words
191	- Heap: remaining free SRAM
192	
193	### Address Unit
194	
195	All addresses are byte addresses. One word = 3 bytes. Offsets in
196	p-code instructions are word indices; multiply by 3 to get byte
197	offsets.
198	
199	## Runtime Helper ABI
200	
201	Runtime helpers are COR24 assembly routines linked with the
202	AOT-compiled program. They follow the standard COR24 calling
203	convention using the hardware stack (sp/r4).
204	
205	### Calling Convention for Helpers
206	
207	Arguments are passed on the eval stack (matching p-code convention).
208	The helper pops its arguments and pushes any return value.
209	
210	| Helper | Args (on eval stack) | Returns | Description |
211	|--------|---------------------|---------|-------------|
212	| `_p24p_write_int` | ( n -- ) | nothing | Print signed integer to UART |
213	| `_p24p_write_bool` | ( flag -- ) | nothing | Print "TRUE" or "FALSE" |
214	| `_p24p_write_str` | ( addr -- ) | nothing | Print null-terminated string |
215	| `_p24p_write_ln` | ( -- ) | nothing | Print newline (0x0A) |
216	| `_p24p_read_int` | ( -- n ) | integer on eval stack | Read integer from UART |
217	| `_p24p_read_ln` | ( -- ) | nothing | Consume remaining input line |
218	| `_p24p_led_on` | ( -- ) | nothing | Turn on LED D2 |
219	| `_p24p_led_off` | ( -- ) | nothing | Turn off LED D2 |
220	
221	### Helper Implementation Notes
222	
223	- `_p24p_write_int` converts integer to decimal ASCII, writes digits
224	  to UART. Handles negative numbers (prefix '-'). Implementation in
225	  `sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/runtime/phase0.spc`.
226	- UART TX: write byte to 0xFF0100; poll 0xFF0101 bit 7 until not busy.
227	- UART RX: poll 0xFF0101 bit 0 until ready; read byte from 0xFF0100.
228	- LED port at 0xFF0000 is active-low (bit 0 inverted in hardware).
229	
230	### AOT-Specific Runtime Helpers (Future)
231	
232	These will be needed as the AOT compiler matures:
233	
234	| Helper | Args | Returns | Description |
235	|--------|------|---------|-------------|
236	| `_rt_bounds_check` | ( idx lo hi -- idx ) | index (unchanged) | Trap if idx < lo or idx > hi |
237	| `_rt_heap_alloc` | ( size -- ptr ) | pointer | Allocate from heap |
238	| `_rt_heap_free` | ( ptr -- ) | nothing | Free heap memory (may be no-op) |
239	| `_rt_div_zero` | ( -- ) | never returns | Trap: division by zero |
240	| `_rt_nil_deref` | ( -- ) | never returns | Trap: nil pointer dereference |
241	| `_rt_stack_overflow` | ( -- ) | never returns | Trap: stack overflow |
242	
243	## Error Behavior
244	
245	### Trap Codes
246	
247	| Code | Name | Trigger |
248	|------|------|---------|
249	| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
250	| 1 | DIV_ZERO | `div` or `mod` with divisor = 0 |
251	| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval stack pointer reaches heap boundary |
252	| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Pop when eval stack is empty |
253	| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unrecognized opcode byte (>= 0x61) |
254	| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Reserved (not implemented in interpreter) |
255	| 6 | NIL_POINTER | `load`/`store`/`loadb`/`storeb` with address = 0 |
256	| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Reserved (not implemented in interpreter) |
257	
258	### Trap Behavior
259	
260	In the interpreter, a trap sets `status=2` and `trap_code=N`, then
261	the VM loop exits and prints `TRAP N\n` followed by a halt loop.
262	
263	For AOT-compiled code, traps should:
264	1. Print an error message identifying the trap type
265	2. Print the approximate source location if available
266	3. Halt execution (enter infinite loop or system halt)
267	
268	### Division Semantics
269	
270	- Division truncates toward zero (standard signed integer division)
271	- Modulo remainder has the same sign as the dividend
272	- Division and modulo by zero always trap (code 1)
273	
274	### Nil Pointer Semantics
275	
276	- Address 0 is the nil pointer
277	- Any dereference of address 0 traps (code 6)
278	- This applies to `load`, `store`, `loadb`, `storeb`
279	
280	### Stack Overflow Detection
281	
282	- Eval stack overflow: checked on every push; compares eval stack
283	  pointer against heap segment base address
284	- Call stack overflow: not explicitly checked in interpreter (implicit
285	  if call stack grows into eval stack)
286	- AOT compiler should check call stack depth at procedure entry
287	
288	## AOT Compilation Considerations
289	
290	### What Changes from Interpreter to AOT
291	
292	| Aspect | Interpreter | AOT |
293	|--------|-------------|-----|
294	| Dispatch | Fetch-decode-execute loop | Native instructions |
295	| Eval stack | Memory array + esp pointer | TOS in r0, NOS in r1, spill to memory |
296	| Call stack | Memory array + csp/fp_vm | Native call frames using fp (r3) |
297	| Code | p-code bytecode in memory | COR24 native instructions |
298	| PC | Software counter (vm_state.pc) | Hardware program counter |
299	| Globals | gp + offset*3 | Fixed addresses resolved at link time |
300	| Data | Embedded after code section | .data section in assembly output |
301	
302	### What Stays the Same
303	
304	- Activation record layout (frame header fields and offsets)
305	- Argument passing convention (eval stack, left-to-right)
306	- Return value convention (pushed to eval stack before ret)
307	- Static link chain traversal for nested scopes
308	- Runtime helper interface (same symbols, same stack protocol)
309	- Memory-mapped I/O addresses
310	- Trap semantics and error behavior
311	- Word size (3 bytes / 24 bits)
312

# COR24 ABI for AOT-Compiled P-Code

Defines the binary interface between AOT-compiled Pascal programs and
the COR24 runtime. This is the contract that both the AOT compiler and
runtime helpers must respect.

Source: `sw-cor24-pcode/vm/pvm.s`, `sw-cor24-pcode/vm/design.md`,
`sw-cor24-pcode/vm/architecture.md`

## COR24 Register Assignments

| Register | Name | AOT Purpose |
|----------|------|-------------|
| r0 | -- | TOS cache / scratch / return value |
| r1 | -- | NOS cache / scratch |
| r2 | -- | Scratch |
| r3 | fp | Frame pointer (points to current activation record) |
| r4 | sp | Stack pointer (COR24 hardware stack in EBR) |
| r5 | z | Zero register (always 0, read-only) |
| r6 | iv | Interrupt vector (reserved) |
| r7 | ir | Interrupt return (reserved) |

### Register Convention

- **r0-r2**: Caller-saved. Any call may destroy them.
- **r3 (fp)**: Callee-saved. Callee must restore before return.
- **r4 (sp)**: Callee-saved (balanced push/pop).
- **r5 (z)**: Read-only zero. Never written.
- **r6-r7**: Reserved for interrupt handling. Never used by compiled code.

### TOS Caching Strategy

The interpreter uses a memory-based eval stack. The AOT compiler should
use TOS caching to avoid excessive memory traffic:

- **r0**: Always holds TOS when the eval stack is non-empty
- **r1**: Optionally holds NOS for binary operations
- **r2**: Available as scratch for address computation

When the eval stack depth exceeds 2, spill to the memory-based eval
stack pointed to by the eval stack pointer (a dedicated memory
location, not a COR24 register).

## Activation Record Layout

The call stack grows upward (toward higher addresses). Each procedure
call creates an activation record (frame) on the call stack.

```
HIGHER ADDRESSES (call stack grows up)

                +-------------------+
                | local[N-1]        |  fp_vm + (N-1)*3
                | ...               |
                | local[0]          |  fp_vm + 0
   fp_vm -----> +-------------------+
                | saved_esp         |  fp_vm - 3    (eval stack at call time)
                | dynamic_link      |  fp_vm - 6    (caller's fp_vm)
                | static_link       |  fp_vm - 9    (enclosing scope's fp_vm)
                | return_pc         |  fp_vm - 12   (return address)
                +-------------------+
                | arg[N-1]          |  saved_esp - N*3
                | ...               |
                | arg[0]            |  saved_esp - 3
                | [eval stack TOS]  |  saved_esp
                +-------------------+

LOWER ADDRESSES
```

### Frame Header (12 bytes)

| Offset from fp_vm | Size | Field | Description |
|--------------------|------|-------|-------------|
| -12 | 3 bytes | return_pc | P-code address to resume after return |
| -9 | 3 bytes | static_link | Frame pointer of lexically enclosing scope |
| -6 | 3 bytes | dynamic_link | Caller's frame pointer (for unwinding) |
| -3 | 3 bytes | saved_esp | Eval stack pointer at time of call |

Note: The interpreter stores these as 4-byte values (word-aligned
reads with `lw`/`sw`), but only the low 3 bytes (24 bits) carry data.
For AOT compilation targeting native COR24, these should be 3 bytes
(one COR24 word) each.

### Local Variables

Local variables occupy slots starting at fp_vm, growing upward.
Each local is one word (3 bytes). Accessed as:

```
local[i] = mem[fp_vm + i * 3]
```

### Argument Access

Arguments are pushed by the caller onto the eval stack before the call.
The callee accesses them relative to saved_esp:

```
arg[i] = mem[saved_esp - (i + 1) * 3]
```

Where i=0 is the first argument (pushed first, deepest on stack).

## Calling Convention

### Call Sequence

1. **Caller** pushes arguments left-to-right onto the eval stack
2. **Caller** executes `call target`:
   - Writes frame header to call stack (return_pc, static_link,
     dynamic_link, saved_esp)
   - Advances call stack pointer by 12 bytes
   - Jumps to target
3. **Callee** executes `enter nlocals`:
   - Sets fp_vm = call stack pointer
   - Advances call stack pointer by nlocals * 3
4. **Callee** does work, accessing args via `loada`, locals via `loadl`
5. **Callee** pushes return value (if function) onto eval stack
6. **Callee** executes `ret nargs`:
   - Detects return value: if eval stack pointer > saved_esp
   - Saves return value if present
   - Restores return_pc, dynamic_link from frame header
   - Pops entire frame from call stack
   - Cleans arguments: eval stack pointer = saved_esp - nargs * 3
   - Pushes return value back if present
   - Jumps to return_pc

### Static Link Setup

For nested procedure calls (`calln depth addr`):
- `depth=0`: static_link = current fp_vm (calling a directly nested proc)
- `depth=1`: static_link = current frame's static_link (calling a sibling)
- `depth>1`: follow static chain (depth-1) times

### Nonlocal Variable Access

To access a variable in an enclosing scope (`loadn depth off`):

```
frame = fp_vm
repeat depth times:
    frame = mem[frame - 9]    // follow static_link
result = mem[frame + off * 3]
```

## Memory Regions

### COR24 Address Space (1 MB SRAM + I/O)

```
0x000000  +------------------------+
          | Code segment           |  Native COR24 instructions
          | (AOT-compiled program  |  (replaces p-code bytecode)
          |  + runtime helpers)    |
          +------------------------+
          | Read-only data         |  String literals, constants
          | (.data sections)       |
          +------------------------+
          | Global variables       |  One word per .global declaration
          +------------------------+
          | Call stack             |  Activation records (grows up)
          +------------------------+
          | Eval stack             |  Expression temporaries (grows up)
          +------------------------+
          | Heap                   |  Dynamic allocation (grows up)
          +------------------------+
          | Free SRAM              |
          +------------------------+
0x0FFFFF  | End of SRAM (1 MB)     |
          +------------------------+
0xFEEC00  | EBR (3 KB)             |  COR24 hardware stack (sp/r4)
0xFEF7FF  +------------------------+
0xFF0000  | LED/Switch I/O         |  Write: LED D2; Read: button S2
0xFF0100  | UART data              |  TX/RX byte
0xFF0101  | UART status            |  Bit 7: TX busy; Bit 0: RX ready
```

### Segment Sizing

The interpreter allocates small fixed segments:
- Globals: 8 words (24 bytes)
- Call stack: 32 words (96 bytes)
- Eval stack: 32 words (96 bytes)
- Heap: 32 words (96 bytes)

For AOT-compiled programs, segment sizes should be configurable.
Reasonable defaults for a 1 MB address space:
- Call stack: 256-1024 words
- Eval stack: 128-512 words
- Heap: remaining free SRAM

### Address Unit

All addresses are byte addresses. One word = 3 bytes. Offsets in
p-code instructions are word indices; multiply by 3 to get byte
offsets.

## Runtime Helper ABI

Runtime helpers are COR24 assembly routines linked with the
AOT-compiled program. They follow the standard COR24 calling
convention using the hardware stack (sp/r4).

### Calling Convention for Helpers

Arguments are passed on the eval stack (matching p-code convention).
The helper pops its arguments and pushes any return value.

| Helper | Args (on eval stack) | Returns | Description |
|--------|---------------------|---------|-------------|
| `_p24p_write_int` | ( n -- ) | nothing | Print signed integer to UART |
| `_p24p_write_bool` | ( flag -- ) | nothing | Print "TRUE" or "FALSE" |
| `_p24p_write_str` | ( addr -- ) | nothing | Print null-terminated string |
| `_p24p_write_ln` | ( -- ) | nothing | Print newline (0x0A) |
| `_p24p_read_int` | ( -- n ) | integer on eval stack | Read integer from UART |
| `_p24p_read_ln` | ( -- ) | nothing | Consume remaining input line |
| `_p24p_led_on` | ( -- ) | nothing | Turn on LED D2 |
| `_p24p_led_off` | ( -- ) | nothing | Turn off LED D2 |

### Helper Implementation Notes

- `_p24p_write_int` converts integer to decimal ASCII, writes digits
  to UART. Handles negative numbers (prefix '-'). Implementation in
  `sw-cor24-pascal/compiler/runtime/phase0.spc`.
- UART TX: write byte to 0xFF0100; poll 0xFF0101 bit 7 until not busy.
- UART RX: poll 0xFF0101 bit 0 until ready; read byte from 0xFF0100.
- LED port at 0xFF0000 is active-low (bit 0 inverted in hardware).

### AOT-Specific Runtime Helpers (Future)

These will be needed as the AOT compiler matures:

| Helper | Args | Returns | Description |
|--------|------|---------|-------------|
| `_rt_bounds_check` | ( idx lo hi -- idx ) | index (unchanged) | Trap if idx < lo or idx > hi |
| `_rt_heap_alloc` | ( size -- ptr ) | pointer | Allocate from heap |
| `_rt_heap_free` | ( ptr -- ) | nothing | Free heap memory (may be no-op) |
| `_rt_div_zero` | ( -- ) | never returns | Trap: division by zero |
| `_rt_nil_deref` | ( -- ) | never returns | Trap: nil pointer dereference |
| `_rt_stack_overflow` | ( -- ) | never returns | Trap: stack overflow |

## Error Behavior

### Trap Codes

| Code | Name | Trigger |
|------|------|---------|
| 0 | USER_TRAP | `trap 0` instruction |
| 1 | DIV_ZERO | `div` or `mod` with divisor = 0 |
| 2 | STACK_OVERFLOW | Eval stack pointer reaches heap boundary |
| 3 | STACK_UNDERFLOW | Pop when eval stack is empty |
| 4 | INVALID_OPCODE | Unrecognized opcode byte (>= 0x61) |
| 5 | INVALID_ADDRESS | Reserved (not implemented in interpreter) |
| 6 | NIL_POINTER | `load`/`store`/`loadb`/`storeb` with address = 0 |
| 7 | BOUNDS_CHECK | Reserved (not implemented in interpreter) |

### Trap Behavior

In the interpreter, a trap sets `status=2` and `trap_code=N`, then
the VM loop exits and prints `TRAP N\n` followed by a halt loop.

For AOT-compiled code, traps should:
1. Print an error message identifying the trap type
2. Print the approximate source location if available
3. Halt execution (enter infinite loop or system halt)

### Division Semantics

- Division truncates toward zero (standard signed integer division)
- Modulo remainder has the same sign as the dividend
- Division and modulo by zero always trap (code 1)

### Nil Pointer Semantics

- Address 0 is the nil pointer
- Any dereference of address 0 traps (code 6)
- This applies to `load`, `store`, `loadb`, `storeb`

### Stack Overflow Detection

- Eval stack overflow: checked on every push; compares eval stack
  pointer against heap segment base address
- Call stack overflow: not explicitly checked in interpreter (implicit
  if call stack grows into eval stack)
- AOT compiler should check call stack depth at procedure entry

## AOT Compilation Considerations

### What Changes from Interpreter to AOT

| Aspect | Interpreter | AOT |
|--------|-------------|-----|
| Dispatch | Fetch-decode-execute loop | Native instructions |
| Eval stack | Memory array + esp pointer | TOS in r0, NOS in r1, spill to memory |
| Call stack | Memory array + csp/fp_vm | Native call frames using fp (r3) |
| Code | p-code bytecode in memory | COR24 native instructions |
| PC | Software counter (vm_state.pc) | Hardware program counter |
| Globals | gp + offset*3 | Fixed addresses resolved at link time |
| Data | Embedded after code section | .data section in assembly output |

### What Stays the Same

- Activation record layout (frame header fields and offsets)
- Argument passing convention (eval stack, left-to-right)
- Return value convention (pushed to eval stack before ret)
- Static link chain traversal for nested scopes
- Runtime helper interface (same symbols, same stack protocol)
- Memory-mapped I/O addresses
- Trap semantics and error behavior
- Word size (3 bytes / 24 bits)

    pop     r0

    ; 03B8  addrg 0x000004
    ; TODO: unsupported opcode addrg

    ; 03BC  loadl 0

.L03D6:
    ; 03D6  ret 1

    ; 03D8  leave

.L03D9:
    ; 03D9  enter 1

    sub     sp, 3

    ; 03DB  loadg 0x000002
    la      r1, _p24_globals
    lw      r0, 6(r1)
    push    r0

    ; 03DF  loadg 0x000003
    la      r1, _p24_globals
    lw      r0, 9(r1)

    ; 03E3  sub
    pop     r0

    ; 03E4  storel 0

    ; 03E6  loadl 0

    ; 03E8  push 0x000000
    lc      r0, 0

    ; 03EC  gt
    pop     r0

    ; 03ED  jz 0x000415
    pop     r0

    brf     .Lskip24
    la      r2, .L0415
    jmp     (r2)
.Lskip24:

    ; 03F1  push 0x00004C
    lc      r0, 76

    ; 03F5  sys 1
    ; TODO: unsupported opcode sys

    ; 03F7  push 0x000045

    ; 03FB  sys 1

    lc      r0, 58

    ; 040D  sys 1
    ; TODO: unsupported opcode sys

    la      r2, .L042B

.L0415:
    ; 0415  push 0x00004F
    lc      r0, 79

    ; 0419  sys 1

.L042B:
